title: ADPA Template Lifecycle, Versioning, and AI Feedback Loop Enhancement
problem_statement: ADPA templates are currently managed as static files, lacking structured lifecycle management, robust versioning, and continuous improvement mechanisms. This limits their adaptability, auditability, and ability to leverage AI-driven optimization, resulting in challenges for quality assurance, compliance, and scalable operations.
proposed_solution: Implement a comprehensive template lifecycle and versioning framework with explicit states, auditable transitions, and traceable lineage. Integrate multi-dimensional AI feedback loops for continuous quality audits, optimization, and improvement, governed by human-in-the-loop (HITL) controls and selective, confidence-based automation. Support robust rollback, regeneration, and observability to ensure compliance, quality, and operational scalability.
key_goals: Transform templates into dynamic, continuously improving intelligence assets.
Establish explicit, auditable lifecycle states and versioning for templates and documents.
Enable AI-driven quality audits, optimization, and improvement loops.
Maintain human-in-the-loop governance for high-risk changes, with controlled automation for low-risk updates.
Ensure traceability, rollback capability, and comprehensive observability.
assumptions: ADPA backend and service architecture supports integration of new lifecycle and versioning models.
AI services can provide reliable quality audits and optimization recommendations.
Human reviewers are available for approval of high-risk or structural changes.
Automation thresholds and policies can be defined and tuned based on template class and risk.
Audit, lineage, and observability data can be persisted and queried efficiently.
constraints: All production template changes must pass through gated validation and approval.
Automation is limited to high-confidence, low-risk changes with rollback paths.
Regeneration and rollback operations must preserve document-template lineage.
Bulk operations require concurrency and throttling controls.
Compliance and standards changes require manual approval.
